title: Comprehensive Guide Classification Python INFS3822
body:
INFS3822 Comprehensive Guide in Python including all steps required to build ML Classification Models

This guide summarises all the steps you learned during weeks 3 and 4 including data loading, preprocessing (data cleaning and transformation including the conversion of categorical variables into dummy variables), feature selection (using Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap), splitting data into training and testing datasets, model training and testing to assess the performance of the models. This guide including the steps requires for building ML models for classification problems using logistic regression, decision tree and neural network in Python. First, upload the file data_online_retail_customers.csv into this notebook on Google Colab. Once the dataset is uploaded, you can conduct the following steps.

1 Importing Libraries The following libraries are the ones used during weeks 3 and 4 that cover the required libraries for logistic regression, decision tree, and neural networks. import pandas as pd import numpy as np from tensorflow . keras . models import Sequential from tensorflow . keras . layers import Dense from sklearn . inspection import per mu tat io n_i mpo rt anc e from sklearn . base import BaseEstimator , ClassifierMixin from sklearn . model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn . linear_model import LogisticRegression from sklearn . tree import DecisionTreeClassifier , export_text , plot_tree from sklearn . metrics import accuracy_score from sklearn . metrics import mean_absolute_error , mean_squared_error from sklearn . metrics import confusion_matrix from sklearn . metrics import classi ficat ion_re port from sklearn . utils import class_weight from sklearn import preprocessing import seaborn as sns import seaborn as sns import matplotlib . pyplot as plt from pandas . api . types import is_numeric_dtype , is_categorical_dtype , is_bool_dtype

2 Loading the Data from a CSV File # Load the data data = pd . read_csv ( ' d a t a _ o n l i n e _ r e t a i l _ c u s t o m e r s . csv ')

3 Data Cleaning # Check for missing values missing_values = data . isna () print ( missing_values . any () . any () )

False

# Remove rows with missing values , if any data = data . dropna ()

4 Data Transformation # Check the data type of all variables print ( data . dtypes )

Customer_ID int64 Age int64 Gender object Annual_Income float64 Total_Spend float64 Years_as_Customer int64 Num_of_Purchases int64 Num_of_Returns int64 Num_of_Support_Contacts int64 Satisfaction_Score int64 Last_Purchase_Days_Ago int64 Email_Opt_In bool Promotion_Response object Churned bool dtype: object

# Convert categorical columns ( ' object ' type ) into ' category ' type data [ ' Gender '] = data [ ' Gender ' ]. astype ( ' category ') data [ ' Pr omotion_Response '] = data [ ' Promotion_Response ' ]. astype ( ' category ')

Further data transformations Uncomment the lines you need by removing the # Modify the code to fit your needs # In this dataset , the target variable is Churned , and it has the type ' bool ' ( i . e . boolean ) . # If the target variable is binary ( e . g . Yes / No , Good / Bad , Success / Fail ) , make sure you convert it into boolean variable as follows :

# data [ ' Churned '] = data [ ' Churned '] == ' Yes '

# Convert date columns into datetime based on your objectives . # For further details , refer to W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g # data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to_datetime ( data [ ' vdate ']) # for dates in the format m / d / yyyy # data [ ' vdate '] = pd . to_datetime ( data [ ' vdate '] , dayfirst = True ) # for dates in the format d / m / yyyy

# If you have a variable representing a date , you can transform it into a categorical variable ( Think of what would be the categories that are relevant to your analysis objectives ) # For further details , refer to W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g # Create a categorical variable for seasons # def get_season ( date ) : # month = date . month # if month in [12 , 1 , 2]: # return ' Winter ' # elif month in [3 , 4 , 5]: # return ' Spring ' # elif month in [6 , 7 , 8]: # return ' Summer ' # else : # return ' Autumn ' #
# data [ ' season '] = data [ ' vdate ']. apply ( get_season ) # data [ ' season '] = data [ ' season ']. astype ( ' category ')

# You can transform some data values . For example , convert '5+ ' to numeric 5 in the ' rcount ' column ( W e e k 4 _ P r e _ S e m i n a r _ U s i n g _ P y t h o n _ f o r _ C l u s t e r i n g ) # data [ ' rcount '] = data [ ' rcount ']. replace ( '5+ ' , 5) . astype ( int )

# Scale the numeric input data # Identify numeric columns numeric_columns = data . select_dtypes ( include =[ ' float64 ' , ' int64 ' ]) . columns

# Scale numeric columns scaler = preprocessing . StandardScaler () . fit ( data [ numeric_columns ]) data [ numeric_columns ] = scaler . transform ( data [ numeric_columns ])

5 Feature Selection # Split the data into inputs ( X as features ) and outputs ( y as target variable ) . # Initial feature selection ( i . e . selecting which variables should be included in X ) is required here as follows . # You can exclude variables that represent unique identifiers because they don 't have any impact on the target variable . # You can exclude variables that are text descriptions and not useful as categories . # You can exclude categorical variables that have too many possible values , as these would generate too many dummy variables . A model with too many variables cannot perform well and sometimes cause the machine to run out of memory . This would also help you focus on the most important variables . X = data . drop ( columns =[ ' Customer_ID ' , ' Churned ' ]) y = data [ " Churned " ]

Pair Plot and Correlation Matrix Heatmap # Determine the type of the target variable targe t_is_numeric = is_numeric_dtype ( y ) and not is_bool_dtype ( y ) t a r g e t _ i s _cate gorica l = is_categorical_dtype ( y ) targe t_is_boolean = is_bool_dtype ( y )

target = " Churned "

# Plot each feature against the target variable using appropriate plots in a grid num_features = len ( X . columns ) num_cols = 3 num_rows = ( num_features + num_cols - 1) // num_cols # Ceiling division

fig , axes = plt . subplots ( num_rows , num_cols , figsize =(15 , num_rows * 5) )

# Plot each feature against the target variable for i , feature in enumerate ( X . columns ) : row = i // num_cols col = i % num_cols ax = axes [ row , col ] # Determine the type of the feature fe at ure_is_numeric = is_numeric_dtype ( X [ feature ]) and not is_bool_dtype ( X [ feature ]) fe at ure_is_boolean = is_bool_dtype ( X [ feature ]) f e a t u re _i s_c ate go ric al = is_categorical_dtype ( X [ feature ])

# Select appropriate plot if ( t ar get_is _categ orica l or target_is_boolean ) and feature_is_numeric : sns . boxplot ( x =y , y = X [ feature ] , ax = ax ) ax . set_title ( f ' Box Plot of { feature } by { target } ') elif target_is_numeric and feature_is_numeric : sns . scatterplot ( x = X [ feature ] , y =y , ax = ax ) ax . set_title ( f ' Scatter Plot of { feature } vs { target } ') elif ( targ et_is_ categ orical or target_is_boolean ) and ( feature_is_boolean or f e a t u r e _is _c ate go ric al ) : sns . countplot ( x = X [ feature ] , hue =y , ax = ax ) ax . set_title ( f ' Count Plot of { feature } by { target } ') elif target_is_numeric and ( feature_is_boolean or fea tur e_ is_ ca teg ori ca l ) : sns . boxplot ( x = X [ feature ] , y =y , ax = ax ) ax . set_title ( f ' Box Plot of { target } by { feature } ')

# Remove any empty subplots for i in range ( num_features , num_rows * num_cols ) : fig . delaxes ( axes . flatten () [ i ])

# Adjust layout plt . tight_layout () plt . show ()

# Convert categorical variables into dummy variables to include individual categories as variables . Please make sure that you do this before constructing the correlation heatmap . This would help you identify the most important features to be included in your models . It is also important to do this before training the models . X = pd . get_dummies (X , drop_first = True )

# Let 's plot the correlation matrix heatmap to check which variables are important ( i . e . correlated with the target variable ) . # Compute the correlation matrix corr_matrix = pd . concat ([ X , y ] , axis =1) . corr ()

# Plot the heatmap plt . figure ( figsize =(12 , 8) ) sns . heatmap ( corr_matrix , annot = True , fmt = ' .2 f ' , cmap = ' coolwarm ') plt . title ( ' Correlation Matrix Heatmap ') plt . show ()

# Further feature selection based on the results of pair plots and / or correlation matrix heatmap . # You can exclude variables that are not correlated with the targe variable . # When 2 variables ( other than the target variable ) are highly correlated to each other , you can remove one of them to address multi - colinearity problem ( keep the one that is highly correlated with the target variable ) . X = X . drop ( columns =[ ' Age ' , ' P r o m o t i o n _ R e s p o n s e _ R e s p o n d e d ' ])

6 Split the data into training and testing sets # Split the data into training and testing sets X_train , X_test , y_train , y_test = train_test_split (X , y , test_size =0.2)

7 Train and test a Logistic Regression Model # Train a logistic regression model on the training data . # If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don 't churn ) . # You can enable Class Weight Adjustment ( class_weight = ' balanced ') to adjust the class weights in the logistic regression model to give more importance to the minority class .

model = L ogisticRegression ( class_weight = ' balanced ') model . fit ( X_train , y_train )

# Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model . predict ( X_test )

# Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy = accuracy_score ( y_test , y_pred ) print ( " Accuracy : " , accuracy )

from sklearn . metrics import precision_score , recall_score , f1_score # Calculate precision , recall , and F1 score precision = precision_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ') # or ' micro ', ' weighted ' depending on your need recall = recall_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ') f1 = f1_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ')

print ( f " Precision : { precision } " ) print ( f " Recall : { recall } " ) print ( f " F1 Score : { f1 } " )

# Display feature coefficients coefficients = pd . DataFrame ({ ' Feature ': X . columns , ' Coefficient ': model . coef_ [0] }) print ( coefficients )

Accuracy: 0.4852941176470588 Precision: 0.4887152777777778 Recall: 0.4885964912280702 F1 Score: 0.48484848484848486 Feature Coefficient 0 Annual_Income 0.040005 1 Total_Spend 0.040280 2 Years_as_Customer 0.089918 3 Num_of_Purchases -0.119749 4 Num_of_Returns 0.035770 5 Num_of_Support_Contacts -0.038130 6 Satisfaction_Score -0.024344 7 Last_Purchase_Days_Ago 0.071078 8 Email_Opt_In -0.175361 9 Gender_Male -0.112905 10 Promotion_Response_Unsubscribed -0.281478

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Exercise 2 Logistic Regression - Python” provided on Moodle.

8 Train a Decision Tree Model # Train a decision tree model on the training data # If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don 't churn ) . # You can enable Class Weight Adjustment ( class_weight = ' balanced ') to adjust the class weights in the decision tree model to give more importance to the minority class . # You can also adjust max_depth to decide the depth of the tree . model = D ec isi onT re eCl as sif ie r ( max_depth =4 , class_weight = ' balanced ') model . fit ( X_train , y_train )

# Make predictions on the testing data y_pred = model . predict ( X_test )

# Calculate the accuracy of the model accuracy = accuracy_score ( y_test , y_pred ) print ( " Accuracy : " , accuracy )

from sklearn . metrics import precision_score , recall_score , f1_score # Calculate precision , recall , and F1 score precision = precision_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ') # or ' micro ', ' weighted ' depending on your need recall = recall_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ') f1 = f1_score ( y_test , y_pred , average = ' macro ')

print ( f " Precision : { precision } " ) print ( f " Recall : { recall } " ) print ( f " F1 Score : { f1 } " )

feature_list = X_train . columns # Plot the decision tree plt . figure ( figsize =(30 , 20) ) plot_tree ( model , filled = True , label = ' none ' , feature_names = feature_list , rounded = True , fontsize =8) plt . show ()

# Display the decision tree strcutrue in text form ( easier to read ) tree_rules = export_text ( model , feature_names = list ( feature_list ) ) print ( " \ nTree Structure : " ) print ( tree_rules )

# Display the Feature Importance Scores feature_list = X_train . columns print ( " \ nFeature Importance Scores : " ) for name , importance in zip ( feature_list , model . feature_importances_ ) : print ( f " { name }: { importance :.4 f } " )

Accuracy: 0.5147058823529411 Precision: 0.5325581395348837 Recall: 0.5307017543859649 F1 Score: 0.5120678408349641

Tree Structure: |--- Annual_Income <= 1.54 | |--- Years_as_Customer <= 0.14 | | |--- Num_of_Purchases <= -0.93 | | | |--- Annual_Income <= 0.18

| | | |--- Annual_Income > 0.18 | | | | |--- class: True | | |--- Num_of_Purchases > -0.93 | | | |--- Total_Spend <= 1.05 | | | |--- Total_Spend > 1.05 | |--- Years_as_Customer > 0.14 | | |--- Annual_Income <= 0.96 | | | |--- Num_of_Purchases <= 0.60 | | | | |--- class: True | | | |--- Num_of_Purchases > 0.60 | | |--- Annual_Income > 0.96 | | | |--- Total_Spend <= -1.01 | | | |--- Total_Spend > -1.01 | | | | |--- class: True |--- Annual_Income > 1.54 | |--- Annual_Income <= 1.56 | | |--- Years_as_Customer <= -1.48 | | | |--- class: False | | |--- Years_as_Customer > -1.48 | | | |--- class: False | |--- Annual_Income > 1.56 | | |--- Last_Purchase_Days_Ago <= 0.12 | | | |--- class: False | | |--- Last_Purchase_Days_Ago > 0.12 | | | |--- class: True

Feature Importance Scores: Annual_Income: 0.4263 Total_Spend: 0.2522 Years_as_Customer: 0.0670 Num_of_Purchases: 0.1546 Num_of_Returns: 0.0000 Num_of_Support_Contacts: 0.0000 Satisfaction_Score: 0.0000 Last_Purchase_Days_Ago: 0.0999 Email_Opt_In: 0.0000 Gender_Male: 0.0000 Promotion_Response_Unsubscribed: 0.0000

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 4 Further Hands-On Practice - Decision Tree in Python” provided on Moodle.

9 Train a Neural Network Model # Please ensure that all variables are converted into the float32 data type , as TensorFlow and Keras neural networks expect numeric values in float32 format as input . X_train = X_train . astype ( ' float32 ') y_train = y_train . astype ( ' float32 ') X_test = X_test . astype ( ' float32 ') y_test = y_test . astype ( ' float32 ')

# If your dataset is highly imbalanced , the model might predict the majority class for all instances . # An imbalanced dataset for a binary target variable like " Churn " means that the number of cases of one class ( customers who churn ) is much smaller or much larger than the number of cases of the other class ( customers who don 't churn ) . # You can enable Class Weight Adjustment ( class_weight = class_weights_dict ) to adjust the class weights in the neural network model to give more importance to the minority class . # Calculate class weights class_weights = class_weight . compute_class_weight ( ' balanced ' , classes = np . unique ( y_train ) , y = y_train ) # Convert class weights to a dictionary cl as s_ wei ghts_dict = dict ( enumerate ( class_weights ) )

# Define the model model = Sequential () model . add ( Dense (12 , input_shape =( len ( X_train . columns ) ,) , activation = ' relu ') ) model . add ( Dense (6 , activation = ' relu ') ) model . add ( Dense (1 , activation = ' sigmoid ') )

# Compile the model model . compile ( loss = ' binary_crossentropy ' , optimizer = ' adam ' , metrics =[ ' accuracy ' ])

# Train the model model . fit ( X_train , y_train , epochs =5 , batch_size =200 , class_weight = cl as s_ weights_dict )

# Evaluate the model _ , accuracy = model . evaluate ( X_test , y_test ) print ( ' Accuracy : %.2 f ' % ( accuracy *100) )

# Make class predictions with the model predictions = ( model . predict ( X_test ) > 0.5) . astype ( int )

# Print confusion matrix print ( confusion_matrix ( y_test , predictions ) )

print ( c l a ssi ficati on_re port ( y_test , predictions ) )

# Evaluate feature importance using Permutation Importance # Wrapper for Keras model class K e r as Cla ssi fi erW ra ppe r ( BaseEstimator , ClassifierMixin ) : def __init__ ( self , model ) : self . model = model

def fit ( self , X , y ) : self . model . fit (X , y , epochs =1 , batch_size =50 , verbose =0)

return self

def predict ( self , X ) : return ( self . model . predict ( X ) > 0.5) . astype ( int )

def score ( self , X , y ) : _ , accuracy = self . model . evaluate (X , y , verbose =0) return accuracy

# Create an instance of the wrapper wrapped_model = Ke ra sCl ass if ier Wr app er ( model )

# Calculate permutation importance results = pe rmu ta tio n_i mp ort an ce ( wrapped_model , X_test , y_test , scoring = ' accuracy ' , n_repeats =3 , random_state =42)

# Summarize feature importance for i in range ( X_test . shape [1]) : print ( f ' Feature : { X_test . columns [ i ]} , Importance : { results . importances_mean [ i ]:.4 f } ')

Epoch 1/5 3/3 [=======================] - 2s 15ms/step - loss: 0.7667 - accuracy: 0.5185 Epoch 2/5 3/3 [=======================] - 0s 10ms/step - loss: 0.7584 - accuracy: 0.5204 Epoch 3/5 3/3 [=======================] - 0s 8ms/step - loss: 0.7497 - accuracy: 0.5167 Epoch 4/5 3/3 [=======================] - 0s 8ms/step - loss: 0.7429 - accuracy: 0.5204 Epoch 5/5 3/3 [=======================] - 0s 7ms/step - loss: 0.7358 - accuracy: 0.5241 5/5 [=======================] - 1s 7ms/step - loss: 0.8273 - accuracy: 0.3824 Accuracy: 38.24 5/5 [=======================] - 0s 8ms/step [[34 26] [58 18]] precision recall f1-score support

0.0 0.37 0.57 0.45 60 1.0 0.41 0.24 0.30 76

accuracy 0.38 136 macro avg 0.39 0.40 0.37 136 weighted avg 0.39 0.38 0.37 136

5/5 [==============================] - 0s 8ms/step Feature: Annual_Income, Importance: -0.0196 Feature: Total_Spend, Importance: -0.0515 Feature: Years_as_Customer, Importance: -0.0245 Feature: Num_of_Purchases, Importance: -0.0539 Feature: Num_of_Returns, Importance: -0.0441 Feature: Num_of_Support_Contacts, Importance: -0.0588 Feature: Satisfaction_Score, Importance: -0.0343 Feature: Last_Purchase_Days_Ago, Importance: -0.0343 Feature: Email_Opt_In, Importance: -0.0196 Feature: Gender_Male, Importance: -0.0049 Feature: Promotion_Response_Unsubscribed, Importance: -0.0294

To interpret the results (performance of the model and feature importance), please refer to the PDF “Answers to Week 3 Hands-On Deep Dive - Deep Learning in Python” provided on Moodle.
